title: More than Just Algebra
summary: Mrs. Eggleston discusses the importance of relationships in education and the challenge of helping students see algebra beyond the futures they already know.
intro: Canaseraga Central School serves fewer than 200 students PK-12. In this conversation, high school math teacher Mrs. Eggleston reflects on what it means to teach in a place where classes are small enough that teachers know not just how students learn, but who they are outside the classroom as well.

questions.q: What does teaching here look like day-to-day?
questions.a: So I'm from Jasper and I went to Jasper-Troupsburg, so I was familiar myself, kind of with like small school, small district, even Canaseraga even smaller than J-T, so I was kind of even shocked at that when I applied and got the 6th grade job, and, I mean, you're the only sixth grade teacher. So something that immediately was apparent was you don't have even one other teacher at your grade level to kind of like keep pace with or bounce ideas off over, do a project together. Like, you're the whole grade level where like, now I'm the whole algebra and the whole algebra II. So that is sometimes a blessing and a curse. Like, you're not really accountable to somebody else. which can be good, but also there are times when it's kind of like I'm doing Regents exam review right now and I wonder, is this the best strategy that I'm using? Like, what do other people do and I don't really have anybody to ask so that is a challenge. Day to day, I would say the best, the best part about it and what I think makes small districts more successful is that you you cannot help but get to know your kids even more, probably than you ever want to about them. But like the current ninth grade was the last class that I had in 6th grade. So when I decided to move up to math, I had them again in 7th again in 8th. And I will have half of them next year for the two-year track and I'll get the other half back the following year for algebra 2. So, I mean, that's going on five and six years in a row with the same kids. So, in that particular group of kids, it's like, I just know them. Like, I can look at a lesson and instantly say, you know, this student is going fly through this. I'm going to have to make sure they have something else to do at the end. This student's going to struggle on two, five, and 7A. Like, seriously down to the kid down to the bush. Like, you can anticipate where their mind's going to go, what they're going to write, the mistake they're going to make, how to fix it. Like, it's, and that's, it's crazy to me. Like, even growing up in JT, would we have, you know, like a class size of 20. Like, I have a class size of five a lot of the time. So you really can know their strengths, their weaknesses, you understand their lives outside of school, and how that affects their achievement in school.

questions.q: How much does that personal connection affect students academically, especially students who may not see themselves as strong math students?
questions.a: A ton. I even, I've always said that from sixth grade. Like, I know in college, you spend a lot of time with writing lesson plans and all the logistics of things, but you don't get a lot of time spent on things like building relationships, connections, classroom management, things like that, because there is often this trap of teachers who come in and they're new and they want to be liked and they want to form relationships, but so the management piece kind of comes at the expense of that. So then you have classes that get kind of wild and crazy because the teacher just wants to be popular versus setting boundaries. Then you have teachers who I think the boundaries are a bit harsh, they're strict, and then that kind of prevents the relationship from forming, and the cases that just are kind of like, oh, this person's a jerk, I'm not going to do work for them. I think you have to have a middle ground, but if you can establish it, I mean, I have kids in here that you just,I guess I always think of it as like, I have to earn their respect. So once you have it, they will work much harder for you. Like, they will. Even on days when I'm out, I mean, the kids just know how the room is. They know me as well as I know them, they know me, so they know my expectations. They know if there's a sub in here and somebody, one class leaves the room a mess like they know where to put things and then I'm going to want to come in and the room be clean and then I would be upset if it wasn't. But I think they work harder in class for sure when they know you know, I guess if they can see the effort that's going into the lesson getting the obligation, if they respect you, they'll respect that you worked on that and so then they'll work for you. Like they'll know Mrs. E is going to be so disappointed if I did bad on this because she knows I can do better. And so they'll do that much more work where you also kind of hear snippets of their conversations, you'll hear them kind of talk about other classes and like, you know, I was absent for three days, I'm going to do all this teacher's work first, and I don't care about this teacher's work kind of thing, but their attitudes about their teachers have an impact. Kids work better for teachers that they like, for sure.

questions.q: For students who ask, "When am I ever going to use this?", what do you do to make math feel more relevant?
questions.a: I think as the years go on, the fact that you care about them, like, they know it, and they just kind of becomes more familiar, so it's less present in their mind. Like, the older they get, and the higher the stakes get with the exams and things, the more they're kind of like, well, this this stupid, why do I have to do it? Why do I have to pass this test? So there are times when like I'll adjust my curriculum to put more relevant situations in. You know, like math definitely has a stigma for, like, the word problems are crazy unrealistic and unrelatable. So I will adjust those and like make them about the kids themselves, like give them a question, put their name in it, things like that to keep them engaged. I think one area that is lacking when it comes to rural education is making that connection. So for a kid who kind of has their mind made up, it's like, well, my dad's a welder and he makes good money, so I'm gonna be a welder when I graduate and I don't need this. Like, I think bringing in more just, like, education about careers and even careers outside of your immediate town where you live would really help them to see that they would be capable of that. Like, I just feel like the kids, a lot of the kids that I teach aren't thinking of, like, what happens after high school. It's not in their brain. It's either I'm a college kid or I'm a workforce kid. There's no, like, well, let me actually see what's out there. Like, what are jobs there? Like, I just feel like their view of that is so narrow and so localized and so it is often really hard to get them to see where a lot of like, upper level algebra for sure, like where is that going to be relevant in my life? Because they just don't have a concept of where that information could actually be useful. So a lot of the way that I approach it, a lot of the times it's kind of like, well, it doesn't matter what you do with your life. At certain times you're going to run into challenges, like challenging tasks where you have to think them through, you have to problem solve, you have to do something you don't want to do. And for you right now as a 14 year old, algebra might be one of those things and you have to still give it your best effort and think it through and solve the problem. Like, if you think you know exactly where your life's going to go and what your job's going to be, like, you might have to pass a certification exam, Work with someone you don't like or there's always going to be a challenge that you have to take through. So I try to approach it that way, but I think if they had more of a concept of just careers in general outside of him that would help. Sometimes I see that when these kids talk about certain careers, it's not that they're super passionate about it or they love it, it's more of like, this is what my dad does so this is what I'll do. So I wish that there was more exposure to just see their options.

title: The Advantage of Small Classes
summary: Mrs. Eggleston discusses how small classes can change the way students learn, interact, and push each other.
intro: Canaseraga Central School serves fewer than 200 students PK-12. In this conversation, high school math teacher Mrs. Eggleston reflects on how very small classes shape learning, accountability, and the way students work together.

questions.q: Earlier, you mentioned that smaller schools can be more successful. What do you mean by that?
questions.a: Well, every year we look at our test grades and compare them to the greater BOCES region. And I just think it always comes out that like, I think it's Canaseraga and Bradford is another super small district where the scores are always just kind of soaring compared to a lot of, you know, like bigger schools like Bath or Horseheads or Hornell, things like that. And I think part of that makes sense of like, at bigger schools you're going to just have a wider variety of kids. That's harder to meet the needs there. But you also have a lot more resources. So for me in algebra, one, like, those Regents scores are only mine compared to Hornell, which might have like three different teachers who contributed to those scores, and that's a lot of more knowledge and resources that they can devote and, you know, collaborate versus just me here. And then, of course, our numbers are skewed because where they might have hundreds of kids taking the same test, I've got 11. It can be good or bad, because if two kids don't do well, then my grades are just not going to look great. But it just seems consistently that the small schools outperform the larger schools. And I think that's because you just have such a smaller chance of falling through the cracks. Like, if I had a class of 24 in here, one or two kids missing school here and there is kind of like a, okay, that's normal. I have a class of five and my one kid isn't here for three days, like, it's very apparent. And also, I can easily track that kid down and get them caught up one on one. So, and the teachers here are really good about working together on that. Like, there's not many of us, especially in the high school, so communicating with, you know, this one kid is currently absent. Everyone's kind of fighting for his or her time when we get them caught up. Like we are really good at working out systems for that. And so I think just that, just physically being present is noticeable. And then, of course, if I have a class and five kids, like we talked about before, I know them very well, but I can easily pinpoint where they're struggling and give them more time and attention than I could if I had a much bigger group. So I just think the advantage is a lot more one-on-one time with your teacher, just kind of like centralized help specific to you.

questions.q: What role do very small classes, like a class of five, play in academics? Do you see students becoming closer, helping each other, or pushing each other more in that setting?
questions.a: I do. It's interesting to look at the dynamics between like the five kids that I have in here at a time versus when the global teacher has the whole class together. Like she sees, I have two students that always want to work together, always help each other. And it's kind of like a friendly competition-based thing, but they'll help each other too. But in global, they don't even speak to each other. So it's kind of like I definitely see it in here. They form these kind of like miniature friendships and bonds that might not exist outside of the algebra classroom, but in here they will help each other and work together. And I think that is cool. And it's kind of, I kind of like the competition part of it. Like, they're not competing for anything. It's really more like with each other. But they'll push each other, which I appreciate because I kind of feel like that's also kind of a downside with the small school like this is usually, you know, you'll have one or two kids who are way top of their class and everyone else is like miles behind and some of those kids kind of just feel like, I'm just not a smart kid. I'm just not toppling my class and they kind of give up. But when I have them in here in a really small class, they will really compete with each other and it's a good environment.
